content:
# Leviticus Chapter 17 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)

**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 68r–68v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_017_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_017_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** The LORD spoke to Moses: **2** "Speak to Aaron and his sons and to all the Israelites, and give them this command — this is what the LORD has ordered:

**3** "Any Israelite who slaughters an ox or a sheep or a goat — whether inside the camp or outside it — **4** and does not bring it to the entrance of the tent of meeting to present it as an offering to the LORD: that man is guilty of bloodshed. He has shed blood. He shall be cut off from his people.

**5** "The purpose of this is so that the Israelites will bring to the LORD the animals they slaughter in the open country. They must bring them to the priest, at the entrance to the tent of meeting, and offer them as fellowship offerings to the LORD. **6** The priest shall splash the blood against the altar of the LORD at the entrance to the tent of meeting and burn the fat as a pleasing aroma to the LORD. **7** They must no longer offer their sacrifices to the goat-demons — the spirits to whom they have been prostituting themselves. This shall be a permanent statute for them, binding on all their generations.

**8** "Tell them: Any Israelite — or any foreigner living among you — who offers a burnt offering or a sacrifice **9** and does not bring it to the entrance of the tent of meeting to offer it to the LORD: that person shall be cut off from their people.

**10** "Any Israelite — or any foreigner living among you — who eats any blood: I will set my face against that person who eats blood, and I will cut them off from their people. **11** For the life of the flesh is in the blood. I myself have given it to you on the altar to make atonement for your lives. It is the blood — because it carries life — that makes atonement.

**12** "Therefore I have said to the Israelites: No one among you shall eat blood. No foreigner living among you shall eat blood.

**13** "Any Israelite — or any foreigner living among you — who hunts and catches an animal or a bird that may be eaten: they shall drain out its blood and cover it with dirt. **14** For the life of every creature is its blood — the blood is its life. And so I said to the Israelites: You must not eat the blood of any creature, because the life of every creature is its blood. Anyone who eats it shall be cut off.

**15** "Anyone — native-born or foreigner — who eats an animal that died on its own or was torn by wild beasts shall wash their clothes, bathe in water, and be unclean until evening; then they will be clean. **16** But if they do not wash or bathe their body, they shall bear the consequences of their sin."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### The Chapter That Commands the Altar

Leviticus 17 is a pivot chapter. It stands at the boundary between the great ritual law of chapters 1–16 and the holiness code of chapters 18–27. Its subject is blood — where it must flow, what it must never be used for, and why. And at the exact center of the chapter, in verse 11, the entire sacrificial theology of the Torah is compressed into a single sentence: *the life of the flesh is in the blood, and I have given it to you on the altar to make atonement for your lives.*

This is not a peripheral comment. It is the architectonic statement on which the entire sacrificial system rests. Everything before it in Leviticus 1–16 — all the careful descriptions of burnt offerings and grain offerings and peace offerings and sin offerings and guilt offerings, all the regulations of Day of Atonement blood-ritual — is preparation for this verse. And everything after it in the New Testament's theology of atonement — Paul's "justified by his blood" (Romans 5:9), the Letter to the Hebrews' great extended argument about the blood of Christ as the final and sufficient sacrifice — takes this verse as its axiomatic foundation.

### Goat-Demons: What Israel Was Actually Doing

The command to centralize all sacrifice at the tabernacle (vv.3–7) is motivated in verse 7 by something startling: the Israelites have been offering their field sacrifices to *se'irim* — goat-demons, hairy ones, wilderness spirits. This is not a hypothetical prohibition against a temptation they might face in the future. The Hebrew is in the ongoing present tense: they *have been* prostituting themselves to these entities. The centralization law exists because actual idolatry is already happening.

The word *se'irim* (singular: *sa'ir*) appears elsewhere in the Hebrew Bible in contexts that make clear these are not merely symbolic constructs. Isaiah 13:21 and 34:14 describe them haunting the ruins of Babylon and Edom. The Leviticus regulation thus does something very precise: it does not deny that there are other spiritual entities to which animals might be offered in the field. It forbids offering to them. The exclusivity of Israel's worship is not based on the non-existence of other powers; it is based on the command of the one God to whom all worship is owed.

The word translated "prostituting themselves" (*zanah*) is the covenant-infidelity word — the same word used throughout the prophets for Israel's relationship with foreign gods. To offer blood to the goat-demons is not merely wrong practice; it is marital betrayal. This is why the legislation is phrased with such severity.

### Blood Guilt for Slaughter Outside the Camp

Verses 3–4 create a startling equation: slaughtering an animal outside the camp without bringing it to the tent of meeting is treated as *bloodshed* — the same word used for killing a human being. The field-slaughterer "has shed blood" (*dam shafakh*). He is guilty of blood.

This seems extreme until the underlying logic is grasped. All animal life belongs to God. When an animal's blood is shed in ritual, the blood is returned to God at the altar; the life is offered back to the life-giver, and atonement flows from that transaction. But when the blood is shed in the field — outside the altar's reach, outside the priestly mediation, without the God-appointed ritual — the blood is simply wasted. Worse, it may be offered to the *se'irim*. The life-blood that belongs to God is being diverted to unauthorized claimants. From the text's perspective, this is a form of violence against the order of creation.

The law here is not purely about hygiene or social control; it is a claim about ontology. Blood is not merely a biological fluid. It is the carrier of *nefesh* — the animating life-principle that God himself breathed into every living thing. To treat it carelessly is to treat the gift of life carelessly.

### Verse 11: The Architecture of Atonement

The verse that gives this chapter its theological weight deserves slow, close attention:

> *ki nefesh ha-basar ba-dam hi, va-ani netattiv lakhem al-ha-mizbe'ach le-khapper al-nafshoteikhem, ki ha-dam hu ba-nefesh yekhapper.*

Broken into its three movements:

**Movement 1: "The life of the flesh is in the blood."** The word *nefesh* does not map neatly onto the English "life" or "soul." It is the animating breath-principle, the vitality that distinguishes a living thing from a dead one. In the ancient Israelite understanding, this *nefesh* — this aliveness — is not located in the brain or the heart in the way we might modernity suppose. It is *in the blood*. This is not a biochemical claim; it is a cosmological one. Blood is the medium of life. When blood drains out, life departs with it. This is what everyone in the ancient world knew by watching animals die, watching soldiers die, watching people bleed out. The two are inseparable: blood and *nefesh*, blood and life.

**Movement 2: "I myself have given it to you on the altar to make atonement for your lives."** The grammatical emphasis on "I" (*va-ani*) is crucial and too easily passed over. God is the subject of this giving. The blood that atones is not something Israel invented, earned, or generates by its own effort. God gave it. This is a profoundly counter-intuitive move. In most ancient religious frameworks, sacrifice is what humans offer *upward* to the deity — a gift, a tribute, a placation. But in Leviticus 17:11 the direction is reversed: God is the one who gives the blood. Israel's atonement is made possible by divine provision, not human initiative.

This is the sentence Paul is standing on in Romans 3:24–25: "justified freely by his grace through the redemption that came by Christ Jesus, whom God put forward as a *hilasterion* [mercy seat / place of propitiation] by his blood." The movement is the same — *God put forward, God gave* — and the blood is the means. The apostle is not inventing a new theology; he is drawing out the logic already present in this verse.

**Movement 3: "It is the blood — because it carries life — that makes atonement."** This final clause (literally "the blood atones by means of the life/soul in it") clarifies the mechanism: blood atones not merely because it is a costly offering, not merely because it satisfies some abstract legal principle, but because it carries *nefesh* — life itself. A life is given in place of a life. The animal's *nefesh*, present in its blood, offered on the altar, stands in the place of the human *nefesh* that stands under sentence. This is the lex talionis of atonement: life for life, not in retribution but in substitution.

The implication for the blood-prohibition that follows (vv.10, 12, 14) is now clear. If blood carries *nefesh* — if blood is the physical medium of life — then consuming blood is not just a ritual violation. It is a claim over life itself, a usurpation of what belongs to God. Only God may direct where that life-force goes. It may not be eaten, absorbed into a human body, as if the consumer could thereby acquire the life contained in it. It must be poured out, returned to the earth (in the case of hunted game, v.13) or offered on the altar (in the case of sacrificial animals). Life returns to its source.

### The Foreigner at Every Verse

One of the quiet structural features of this chapter is that every major prohibition mentions the foreigner (*ger* — the resident alien) alongside the Israelite (vv.8, 10, 12, 13, 15). This is not incidental. The blood-theology of verse 11 is not an in-group covenant secret; it is a statement about the nature of life itself. The *nefesh* is in the blood for every creature, not only for Israelite creatures. The prohibition on eating blood therefore extends to everyone living within the community, because it is grounded in a claim about creation, not merely about Israel's particular covenant obligations.

This universalizing logic will surface again in the Noahide laws (Genesis 9:4–6), where the blood prohibition is tied to the image of God in every human being. The argument runs: human blood, in particular, may not be shed carelessly, because humans bear the divine image; and no blood may be eaten, because blood carries life, and life belongs to God. Leviticus 17 inhabits the same cosmological framework.

### The LXX: A More Cautious Rendering

The Septuagint (Codex Vaticanus, pp. 103–104) renders this chapter straightforwardly, following the Hebrew closely. Its most significant choice is in verse 7: the Greek uses *mataia* — "worthless things," "vanities" — for the *se'irim*, the goat-demons. This is a demythologizing move: the LXX translators preferred to characterize these entities as non-realities rather than preserve the Hebrew's implication that they are actual spiritual entities to which unauthorized worship was being directed. The Hebrew treats the goat-demons as real threats; the LXX assimilates them to the prophetic tradition of dismissing idols as empty nothings (cf. Isaiah's repeated use of *hebel*, "vapor," for idols).

In verse 11, the LXX renders *nefesh* as *psyche* — the standard Greek equivalent — and *kapparah* (atonement) as *exilasasthai*, a compound of the *hilasmos* word-group that encompasses both propitiation and expiation. This is the same word-group from which *hilasterion* comes — the word Paul will use in Romans 3:25 for Christ as the "mercy seat." The LXX's Leviticus 17:11 is thus one of the direct verbal links in the chain that connects the Mosaic blood-theology to the New Testament's theology of the cross.

### The Life of Every Creature

Verse 14 restates the principle with a subtle variation: "the life of every creature is its blood — the blood is its life." The phrase "every creature" (*kol basar* — literally "all flesh") extends the blood-equals-life equation beyond sacrificial animals to every living thing. This is the most expansive statement of the principle in the chapter. The *nefesh* is not a special property of ritually clean animals or of human beings; it belongs to the blood of *every* creature.

The implication is profound: there is no living thing whose blood is simply biological waste product. Every living thing carries, in its blood, the *nefesh* that God gave it. To handle blood — any blood — is to handle life, and life is God's property. This does not mean Leviticus anticipates modern animal rights frameworks, but it does mean that the text's vision of the world is one in which the line between the sacred and the ordinary is far less sharp than modern secular intuitions suppose. Blood is not inert. It pulses with *nefesh*. And *nefesh* belongs to God.

---
*PNEUMA — translated from Leningrad Codex (B19A) folios 68r–68v, with reference to Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) pages 103–104 (LXX).*
